segments.text: Typically, the problem with vanishing gradients is, like, let's say we have a prediction
task where we're calculating a regression.
We're inputting a whole bunch of tokens and then calculating a regression at the very
end using a square error loss function.
If we do something like this, the problem is, if we have a standard RNN, when we do back
prop, we'll have a big gradient, probably for the first RNN unit here.
But every time because we're running this through some sort of nonlinearity, if we, for
example, if our nonlinearity is a tanH function, the gradient of the tanH function looks a
little bit like this.
And here, if I am not mistaken, this peaks at 1 and everywhere else at 0.
And so, because this is peaking at 1, everywhere else at 0, let's say we have an input way over
here, like minus 3 or something like that.
If we have that, that basically destroys our gradient.
Our gradient disappears for that particular unit.
And maybe one thing that you might say is, oh, well, if this is getting so small because
this only goes up to 1, let's do like 100 times tanH.
And as our activation function, we'll do 100 times tanH.
So now this goes up to 100 and now our gradients are not going to disappear.
But then you have the opposite problem.
You have exploding gradients where it goes up by 100 every time.
It gets on the managed mole and destroys your gradient descent itself.
So basically, we have this problem because if you apply a function over and over again,
your gradient gets smaller and smaller every smaller and smaller and bigger and bigger every
time you do that.
You have the vanishing gradient or exploding gradient problem.
It's not just a problem with nonlinearities.
So it also happens when you do your weight matrix multiplies and other stuff like that.
Basically, anytime you modify the input into a different output, it will have a gradient
and so it will either be bigger than 1 or less.
So I mentioned this is a problem for RNNs.
And it's particularly a problem for RNNs over long sequences.
But it's also a problem for any other model you use.
And the reason why this is important to know is if there's important information in your
model, finding a way that you can get a direct path from that important information to wherever
you're making a prediction often is a way to improve your model, improve your model performance.
And on the contrary, if there's unimportant information, if there's information that you
think is likely to be unimportant, putting it farther away or making it a more
indirect path.
So the model has to work harder to use it is a good way to prevent the model from being
distracted by tons and tons of information, which may be irrelevant.
So it's a good thing to know about in general for a model design.
So how did RNNs solve this problem of the vanishing gradient?
There is a method called Wang short-term memory.
And the basic idea is to make additive connections between time steps.
And so addition is the only addition or kind of like the identity is the only thing that
does not change the gradient.
It's guaranteed to not change the gradient because the identity function is like f of x equals
x.
And if you take the derivative of this, it's one.
So your guaranteed to always have a gradient of one according to this function.
So Wang short-term memory makes sure that you have this additive input between time steps.
And this is what it looks like.
It's not super, super important to understand everything that's going on here, but just
to explain it very quickly.
This C here is something called the memory cell.
It's passed on linearly like this.
And then you have some gates.
The update gate is determining whether you update this hidden state or how much you update
given this hidden state.
This input gate is deciding how much of the input you take in.
And then the output gate is deciding how much of the output from the cell you basically
push out after using the cell.
So I mean, it has these three gates that control the information flow and the model can learn
to turn them on or off or something like that.
So that's the basic basic idea of the LSTM.
And there's lots of other like variants of this like gated recurrent units that are a
little bit simpler.
But the basic idea of an additive connection plus gating is something that appears a lot
in many different types of architectures.
Any questions here?
Another thing I should mention that I just realized I don't have on my slides, but it's a good
thing to know is that this is also used in deep networks and multi-layer networks.
So basically LSTMs, this is time.
LSTMs have this additive connection between the memory cell where you're always adding this
into whatever input you get.
And then you get an input and you add this in.
You get an input.
And so this makes sure you pass your gradients forward in time.
There's also something called residual connections, which I think a lot of people have heard of
if you've done a deep learning class or something like that.
But if you haven't, they're a good thing to know.
Resigual connections are if you run your input through multiple layers.
And if let's say you have a block here, let's call this an RNN for now because we know
about RNN already.
So, this connection here is called the residual connection.
And basically it's adding an additive connection before and after layers.
So this allows you to pass information from the very beginning of a network to the very
end of a network through multiple layers.
And it also is there to help prevent the gradient finishing problem.
So in a way you can view LSTMs, what LSTMs are doing is preventing loss of gradient in time.
And these are preventing loss of gradient as you go through multiple layers of the network.
And this is super standard.
This is used in all like transformer models and LAMA and GPT and whatever else.

So people use to do this usually in combination with attention based models, but they're used
in very long sequence modeling.
Confolutions tend to be used in a lot in speech and image processing.
And the reason why they're used a lot in speech and image processing is because when we're
processing language, we have like this is wonderful.
Like this is wonderful is three tokens in language.
But if we look at it in speech, it's going to be like many, many frames.
So kind of the semantics of language is already kind of like if you look at a single token,
you already get something semantically meaningful.
But in contrast, if you're looking at like speech or you're looking at pixels and images
or something like that, you're not going to get something semantically meaningful.
So convolution is used a lot in that case.
And also you could create a convolutional model of our characters as well.
So what is convolution in the first place?
As I mentioned before, basically you take the local window around an input and you run
it through basically a model.
And a good way to think about it is it's essentially a feed forward network where you can catenate
all of the surrounding vectors together and run them through a linear transform like
this.
So you can catenate xt-1, xt-xt-plus-1.
And convolution can also be used in auto regressive models.
And normally we think of it like this.
So we think that we're taking the previous one, the current one, and the next one in making
a prediction based on this.
But this would be good for something like sequence labeling, but it's not good for something
like language modeling because in language modeling we can't look at the future, right?
